input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The document refers to a building as the physical asset (a cyber‑physical asset) within the Architecture, Engineering, Construction and Operations (AECO) domain, using it as the reference infrastructure for the Digital Twin. The building’s whole life‑cycle—from design through construction, operation, and refurbishment—is considered for digital twinning.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides real‑time insights, predictive analytics, simulation, analysis, and lifecycle‑encompassing optimization to support enhanced decision‑making for the building. Core services include monitoring of operational data, predictive modeling of future performance, scenario simulation, and optimization of building operations across its life‑cycle.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin operates with continuous, real-time synchronization between the physical building and its virtual replica, ingesting runtime sensor data as it is produced to provide real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, and optimization throughout the building lifecycle; the document does not specify exact update frequencies, latency limits, or temporal granularity.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system is orchestrated as a model‑driven framework that automates the deployment of a Digital Twin for a building. A BIM model is processed by a configurator that generates artefacts for Eclipse Hono (IoT connectivity layer) and Eclipse Ditto (digital‑twin state management). Hono collects sensor data and forwards it to Ditto, which maintains the twin’s semantic representation. Visualization is provided by Unreal Engine, which consumes the twin state for interactive 3D rendering. This composition establishes a microservice‑style architecture where Hono, Ditto and the visualisation component are coordinated by the configurator, enabling scalable, automated provisioning and resource management across the DT ecosystem.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The framework uses enriched BIM models as the semantic foundation for the Digital Twin. Building control engineers embed device definitions—sensors, actuators, and controllers—directly into the BIM geometry, creating structured representations that are later extracted via model‑driven engineering (MDE) to generate deployable DT artifacts. Thus the model constellation consists of a geometric BIM base linked to behavioral device descriptions, with data sourced from the physical sensors/actuators and managed through the automatically generated DT services.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Physical actuators and controllers are modeled within the BIM environment and exposed through the Digital Twin for remote operation by facility managers. The document mentions that these actuating devices can be controlled via the DT but does not provide details on actuator types, actuation ranges, safety constraints, or addressing mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are defined as devices in the enriched BIM model; the Digital Twin provides real‑time data exchange to keep the twin synchronized with the physical building. The document does not specify sensor types, measurement capabilities, spatial distribution, sampling frequencies, transmission rates, or accuracy specifications.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The framework provides real‑time data exchange that continuously synchronises physical building sensors and devices with their digital twin. Sensor measurements, actuator states and other operational data are streamed from the physical assets to the DT through a central hub that acts as the integration point. The exchange is described as real‑time and continuous; specific communication protocols, transmission frequencies, event triggers, data‑quality checks or preprocessing steps are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Through the DT, Facility Managers can issue control actions that are sent back to physical equipment for real‑time monitoring and control. The central hub forwards command messages from the digital twin to the building’s devices, supporting immediate feedback. The document does not detail command formats, validation, authentication, conflict resolution, or execution confirmation mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The architecture is modular with standardized extension points and is designed to interoperate across heterogeneous systems, supporting scalability from individual components to whole‑infrastructure deployments. Runtime configuration management allows adjustments without full redeployment. Specific network protocols, topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, or security protocol details are not provided in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The digital twin framework is deployable on a runtime‑configurable infrastructure that can host custom services and analytics, supporting scalability and low‑overhead updates. While the modular design permits deployment on various platforms (cloud, edge, on‑premise), the document does not name particular hosting environments, computational or storage specifications, nor explicit availability or reliability guarantees.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The framework defines a four‑stage lifecycle: (1) device and control‑logic modeling inside the BIM environment where sensors, actuators and controllers are added to the building information model; (2) model‑driven transformation using model‑driven engineering (MDE) to extract structured representations and automatically generate deployable Digital Twin artifacts; (3) deployment and runtime operation where the generated twin is instantiated, kept in continuous sync with the physical plant through real‑time data exchange, and visualised for monitoring and control; (4) evolution stage in which modular extension points allow custom services, analytics or plug‑ins to be added, enabling the twin to adapt to new operational requirements. Transition between stages is managed by the automated generation pipeline and governed by the roles defined (Building Control Engineer, Facility Manager, DT Engineer).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering follows a model‑driven development process. Building Control Engineers enrich BIM models with device definitions and control logic; a model‑driven engineering (MDE) tool extracts these definitions and automatically produces the DT artefacts. DT Engineers develop custom services and plug‑ins that are integrated via the framework’s standardized extension points. The process is highly automated to minimise manual effort, but the document does not specify explicit quality‑assurance, testing, version‑control, or validation practices.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The twin provides real‑time decision support through continuous synchronization of sensor data, interactive 3‑D visualisation, and the ability to invoke custom analytics services. Facility Managers can monitor system states, receive visual cues, and issue control commands directly via the twin interface. Custom analytics extensions can generate higher‑level insights for operational optimisation, although the document does not detail specific analytics algorithms or confidence measures for recommendations.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The architecture is modular with standardized extension points that define how custom services and analytics are plugged into the twin. These extension points constitute the explicit standard referenced; no external industry standards (e.g., IFC, ISO 19650, MQTT) or certification requirements are mentioned in the document.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

The extraction lists specific services such as real-time insights, predictive analytics, simulation, and optimization, which are explicitly mentioned in the source. The text states that DTs enable 'real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization' and provide 'real-time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly lists several specific tools and frameworks used for implementation, including Building Information Modeling (BIM), Eclipse Hono, Eclipse Ditto, and Unreal Engine. Since relevant information exists, this score is 1. Supporting quotes: 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)', 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization'.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The extraction correctly identifies the real-time and continuous nature of the synchronization described in the text, specifically noting the lack of specific numerical metrics (frequencies/latency) which are indeed absent from the source. It captures the general operational tempo: 'real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization' (Abstract), 'live synchronization' (Contributions R3), and 'continuous synchronization with physical assets through sensor data' (Section III).

The source documents describe a framework for deploying 'a Digital Twin' and a 'DT of a Living Lab' but do not discuss multiple digital twin instances, hierarchical structures between twins, or centralized versus decentralized architectures. The text focuses on the deployment of individual instances rather than multiplicities.

The extraction provides a detailed and precise summary of the system's orchestration and architecture. It correctly identifies the model-driven framework, the role of the configurator in processing BIM models to generate artifacts for Eclipse Hono and Eclipse Ditto, the flow of sensor data from Hono to Ditto, and the use of Unreal Engine for visualization.

The source documents discuss 'standardized integration frameworks' and the use of specific middleware (Eclipse Hono and Ditto) for internal data exchange. However, the definition requires describing integration with external enterprise systems or other Digital Twins, and specific protocols. The text focuses on the internal architecture (vertical integration between BIM and runtime) rather than horizontal integration with external peers or enterprise systems, and lacks details on specific data exchange protocols for external entities.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

It correctly identifies enriched BIM models as the foundation, the role of building control engineers in embedding device definitions, and the use of MDE to generate artifacts. Supported by quotes: 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)', 'define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment', and 'automatically generate deployable DT artifacts'.

The extraction correctly describes the modeling of actuators/controllers in BIM and their exposure for remote operation by facility managers. It accurately notes the absence of detailed technical specifications such as safety constraints or actuation ranges in the text. Supported by quotes: 'define... actuators... directly within the BIM environment' and 'Facility Manager... operates the physical infrastructure via the DT'.

The description correctly identifies sensors as devices defined in the BIM model and notes the real-time synchronization capability. It accurately assesses that the document lacks specific technical details like sampling frequencies or accuracy specs. Supported by quotes: 'define sensors... directly within the BIM environment' and 'real-time data exchange'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source text explicitly mentions validating the model and using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric to measure how well the DT reflects its physical counterpart. These are relevant to validation and fidelity. Supported by quotes: 'measuring how well the resulting DT reflects its physical counterpart using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric' and 'after validating the model'.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

The description is well-grounded in the source, accurately detailing the real-time monitoring, visualization capabilities, and the Facility Manager's ability to control the infrastructure. It correctly identifies the provision for custom analytics while noting that specific algorithms are not detailed. Supported by quotes: 'Unreal Engine... provides a rich visualization environment for interactive monitoring', 'Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts with the twin, and operates the physical infrastructure via the DT', and 'supports the integration of custom services and analytics'.

The extraction correctly identifies the 'standardized extension points' as the key standardization feature of the framework. block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
